# T26: Alteration and Damage as Reliability Evidence

Edition: both.

## Input

> This is my transcription of an 1887 family Bible page: "Clara
> Winton born 4 [the 4 is written over another digit] March 1862."
> The surname is faded, the page edge is torn, and a different pencil
> hand added "Aunt" decades later. Can I report the date as exact?

## MUST Criteria

- [ ] Identify overwriting, fading, tearing, and later annotation as
  separate reliability and provenance observations
- [ ] Preserve the observed reading with uncertainty markers rather
  than silently choosing the overwritten digit or supplied surname
- [ ] Distinguish the original entry from the later "Aunt" annotation
- [ ] State that 4 March 1862 is not yet an exact established date
- [ ] Recommend inspection of the original page or better imaging and
  correlation with independent birth, church, census, or death records

## MUST NOT Criteria

- [ ] Must NOT erase, digitally repair, or normalize the alteration
  without disclosure
- [ ] Must NOT present the later annotation as contemporary evidence
- [ ] Must NOT call the uncertain date Proved

## Ground Truth

Alterations, damage, and later hands are evidence about reliability and
provenance, not merely cosmetic defects. The response should preserve
what is observed, mark uncertain readings, separate layers of authorship,
and identify evidence that could clarify the date. The case is invented.
